content:
# Joshua Chapter 22 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 246-247 | **Source Image:** `../../source-texts/hebrew-OT/JOS_022_page_001.jpg`

---

*[The Altar That Almost Started a War — Thirty-four verses of tribal crisis. The eastern tribes — Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh — have honored their pledge to Moses: they crossed the Jordan, fought alongside their brothers, and now Joshua sends them home with his blessing and their share of the plunder. But on the way back they build a massive altar by the Jordan, and word of it nearly tears the young nation apart. The western tribes assume apostasy, muster for war, and send a delegation led by Phinehas the priest to confront them. The eastern tribes explain: the altar is not for sacrifice but for witness — a memorial so that future generations on the west side can never say "You have no share in YHWH." The explanation satisfies. War is averted. The altar is named "Witness," because it stands as testimony between them that YHWH is God. This is a story about unity almost destroyed by misunderstanding, and saved by the willingness to talk before fighting.]*

---

**1** Then Joshua summoned the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh,

**2** and said to them, "You have done everything that Moses, the servant of YHWH, commanded you. And you have obeyed my voice in everything I ordered you to do.

**3** You have not abandoned your brothers — all this long time, right up to today. You have faithfully kept the charge that YHWH your God gave you.

**4** And now YHWH your God has given your brothers rest, just as he promised them. So turn around and go home — back to your own land, the territory that Moses the servant of YHWH gave you on the other side of the Jordan.

**5** Only be very careful to carry out the commandment and the instruction that Moses the servant of YHWH gave you: to love YHWH your God, to walk in all his ways, to keep his commands, to hold fast to him, and to serve him with all your heart and all your being."

**6** Then Joshua blessed them and sent them on their way, and they went back to their tents.

**7** (Moses had given territory in Bashan to one half of the tribe of Manasseh, and Joshua had given the other half territory among their brothers on the west side of the Jordan.) When Joshua sent them off to their tents, he blessed them

**8** and said to them, "Go back to your tents with great wealth — with huge herds of livestock, with silver and gold, with bronze and iron, and with a great quantity of clothing. Divide the plunder from your enemies among yourselves."

**9** So the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh left the other Sons of Israel at Shiloh, which is in the land of Canaan. They set out for the land of Gilead — their own territory, which they had taken possession of at YHWH's command through Moses.

**10** When they reached the region along the Jordan that lies within the land of Canaan, the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh built an altar there by the Jordan — a large, conspicuous altar.

**11** The other Sons of Israel heard about it: "Look — the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh have built an altar at the frontier of the land of Canaan, in the region along the Jordan, on the side belonging to the Sons of Israel!"

**12** When the Sons of Israel heard this, the entire community of the Sons of Israel assembled at Shiloh to go to war against them.

**13** But first, the Sons of Israel sent Phinehas son of Eleazar the priest to the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh in the land of Gilead,

**14** and with him ten leaders — one leader from each ancestral house, from each of the tribes of Israel. Each of them was the head of his ancestral house within the clans of Israel.

**15** They came to the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh in the land of Gilead, and they spoke with them:

**16** "This is what the entire community of YHWH says: 'What is this treachery that you have committed against the God of Israel — turning away from YHWH today by building yourselves an altar, rebelling today against YHWH?

**17** Was the sin of Peor not enough for us? We still have not cleansed ourselves from that to this day, even though a plague struck the community of YHWH because of it.

**18** And now you are turning away from YHWH! If you rebel against YHWH today, then tomorrow his anger will blaze against the entire community of Israel.

**19** If the land you possess is actually unclean, then cross over into YHWH's own territory — where YHWH's tabernacle stands — and take a possession among us. But do not rebel against YHWH, and do not rebel against us, by building for yourselves an altar other than the altar of YHWH our God.

**20** When Achan son of Zerah broke faith regarding what had been set apart for destruction, did not wrath fall on the entire community of Israel? He was not the only one who perished for his sin.'"

**21** Then the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh answered the heads of the clans of Israel:

**22** "The Mighty One! God! YHWH! The Mighty One! God! YHWH! He knows — and Israel itself will know — if this was done in rebellion or in treachery against YHWH, then do not spare us today!

**23** If we built ourselves an altar to turn away from YHWH — if it was for burnt offerings, or grain offerings, or peace offerings — let YHWH himself call us to account.

**24** No! The truth is, we did this out of anxiety — out of a specific concern. We thought, 'Someday your children will say to our children: What do you have to do with YHWH, the God of Israel?

**25** YHWH has set the Jordan as a boundary between us and you — you Reubenites and Gadites! You have no share in YHWH!' And your children will cause our children to stop revering YHWH.

**26** So we said, 'Let us take action and build an altar — not for burnt offerings or sacrifices,

**27** but as a witness between us and you, and between the generations that follow us, that we do perform the service of YHWH before him with our burnt offerings, our sacrifices, and our peace offerings.' Then your children can never say to our children, 'You have no share in YHWH.'

**28** We reasoned: if they ever say that to us or to our descendants in the future, we can reply, 'Look at this replica of YHWH's altar that our ancestors built — not for burnt offerings, not for sacrifice, but as a witness between us and you.'

**29** Far be it from us to rebel against YHWH or to turn away from YHWH today by building an altar for burnt offerings, grain offerings, or sacrifices apart from the altar of YHWH our God that stands before his tabernacle!"

**30** When Phinehas the priest and the leaders of the community — the heads of the clans of Israel who were with him — heard the words that the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the Manassites spoke, they were satisfied.

**31** Phinehas son of Eleazar the priest said to the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the Manassites, "Today we know that YHWH is among us, because you have not committed this treachery against YHWH. Now you have rescued the Sons of Israel from YHWH's hand."

**32** Then Phinehas son of Eleazar the priest and the leaders returned from the Reubenites and the Gadites in the land of Gilead to the Sons of Israel in the land of Canaan, and brought back word to them.

**33** The report pleased the Sons of Israel, and they praised God. They spoke no more of going to war against them to destroy the land where the Reubenites and the Gadites were living.

**34** The Reubenites and the Gadites named the altar "Witness" — "For it is a witness between us that YHWH is God."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 2 — "obeyed my voice"** — Hebrew שְׁמַרְתֶּם אֶת־מִשְׁמֶרֶת (shemartem et-mishmeret) in v. 3 and שְׁמַעְתֶּם בְּקוֹלִי (shema'tem beqoli) here use two different verbs for obedience: שׁמר (keep/guard) and שׁמע (hear/obey). Joshua's farewell commends both their listening and their guarding of the charge.

**Verse 5 — "all your being"** — Hebrew בְּכָל־נַפְשְׁכֶם (bekhol-nafshekhem). The word נֶפֶשׁ (nephesh) means the whole living self — vitality, desire, appetite, life. "Soul" imports later Greek categories; "being" captures the Hebrew sense of one's entire life-force directed toward YHWH.

**Verse 10 — "a large, conspicuous altar"** — Hebrew מִזְבֵּחַ גָּדוֹל לְמַרְאֶה (mizbeach gadol lemar'eh), literally "an altar great to sight/appearance." The phrase emphasizes that this altar was built to be seen — it was a visual statement, which is precisely why it triggered alarm.

**Verse 17 — "the sin of Peor"** — The עֲוֹן פְּעוֹר (avon Pe'or) refers to the incident at Baal-Peor (Numbers 25), where Israelites engaged in sexual worship of Moabite gods and 24,000 died in a plague. Phinehas himself ended that plague by his zeal — which makes his role as lead diplomat here deeply significant. The man who once acted with the spear now comes with words.

**Verse 19 — "YHWH's own territory"** — Hebrew אֶרֶץ אֲחֻזַּת יהוה (eretz achuzzat YHWH). The delegation concedes a remarkable theological point: if the eastern land is ritually impure (טְמֵאָה, teme'ah) because it lacks the tabernacle, they would rather share their own land than let their brothers fall into apostasy. This is a generous offer embedded inside an accusation.

**Verse 22 — "The Mighty One! God! YHWH!"** — Hebrew אֵל אֱלֹהִים יהוה (El Elohim YHWH), repeated twice. This triple divine invocation is an oath formula — the most solemn possible declaration of innocence. The three names pile up for emphasis: El (the Mighty One), Elohim (God), YHWH (the covenant name). The repetition signals the gravity of the moment: they are swearing before the full weight of the divine name.

**Verse 24 — "out of anxiety"** — Hebrew מִדְּאָגָה (midde'agah), from the root דאג meaning to be anxious, fearful, concerned. This is not defensive posturing but genuine existential worry: they feared their descendants would be cut off from the covenant community. The altar was born from fear of future exclusion, not present rebellion.

**Verse 27 — "witness" vs. sacrifice** — The key Hebrew word is עֵד (ed), meaning "witness" or "testimony." The entire crisis resolves around this distinction: the western tribes assumed the altar was a מִזְבֵּחַ (mizbeach) for עֹלוֹת (olot, burnt offerings) and זְבָחִים (zevachim, sacrifices), which would constitute a rival worship site. The eastern tribes clarify it is an עֵד — a monument of testimony, not a place of worship.

**Verse 31 — "rescued... from YHWH's hand"** — Hebrew הִצַּלְתֶּם אֶת־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל מִיַּד יהוה (hitzaltem et-benei Yisra'el miyad YHWH). A striking phrase: the danger was not military defeat but divine punishment. By proving their innocence, the eastern tribes "delivered" all Israel from the consequence of having tolerated rebellion in their midst. Phinehas, who knows what divine wrath looks like (Numbers 25), recognizes what has been averted.

**Verse 34 — the altar's name** — The Hebrew text is compressed here. Most manuscripts read the name as simply עֵד (Ed, "Witness"), though the Leningrad Codex text implies the full explanatory clause: כִּי עֵד הוּא בֵּינֹתֵינוּ כִּי יהוה הָאֱלֹהִים — "for it is a witness between us that YHWH is God." The altar's name is its purpose: testimony to shared allegiance to one God across the river boundary.

**Structural note** — The chapter divides cleanly: vv. 1-8 (Joshua's farewell and blessing), vv. 9-12 (the altar and the crisis), vv. 13-20 (the accusation), vv. 21-29 (the defense), vv. 30-34 (the resolution). The turning point is verse 21, where the accused speak. The narrative rewards diplomacy: Phinehas came ready to condemn but was willing to listen, and listening saved a nation from civil war.
